Any feedback received from residents of disadvantaged areas will be reviewed and evaluated by the Pleasant Valley Subbasin GSAs during the GSP development phase.

V.A.2 Educational/Outreach Public Meetings Stakeholders Educational/outreach public meetings will be scheduled for Phase 2: GSP Preparation and Submission, Phase 3: GSP Review and Evaluation, and Phase 4: Implementation and Reporting (see Section VI for the meeting schedules). These meetings will be important as the GSP will affect all groundwater users within the Pleasant Valley Subbasin GSAs’ jurisdictions, and the impact of the SGMA implementation is significant. Stakeholders are already inquiring about the impacts of implementation, while many stakeholders are unaware of the SGMA. Spanish translation services will be available at educational/outreach public meetings, if needed. • Phase 2: GSP Preparation and Submission – Public meetings held during Phase 2 will be geared towards an overview of SGMA, overview of the GSP development process, public review and implementation (what stakeholders can expect), distribution of stakeholder surveys and solicitation of stakeholder input, and question/answer sessions. This segment of public meetings will give  stakeholders an opportunity to be involved in GSP development and share their thoughts and concerns. Possible venues within the Pleasant Valley Subbasin are listed in Table V-1. Presentations will be given at GSAs’ board and council meetings, and a maximum of two subbasin-wide outreach meetings will be held for stakeholders identified in Table II-2, Table II-3, and Table II-4. • Phase 3: GSP Review and Evaluation – During Phase 3, the draft of the Pleasant Valley Subbasin GSP will be distributed for public review. During the public review period, public meetings will be held at the same venues as during Phase 2 (Table V-1). The presentation will include an overview of the GSP and will give stakeholders the opportunity to comment on the draft in a public forum. Presentations will be given at government agency meetings, and at maximum of two subbasin-wide outreach meetings will be held for stakeholders. At the conclusion of the 90-day public review period, a subbasin-wide public hearing will be held for stakeholders to provide oral and/or written comments before the GSP is finalized. The public hearing will be noticed in local newspapers 90 days and 45 days prior. • Phase 4: Implementation & Reporting – Public meetings will be crucial during Phase 4 and will likely be ongoing to educate stakeholders on implementation requirements and guide them through the steps to compliance and groundwater sustainability.

Printed materials will incorporate the visual imagery established through branding efforts and will be tailored for specific means of communication throughout the phases of GSP development, public review, and implementation. Printed materials will be translated into Spanish, if necessary, for a specific target audience. 
• Fliers – Fliers may be designed and tailored for secondary stakeholder audiences and will encompass infographics and text with key messages that are pertinent for that phase of GSP development. Distribution may be via website posting, direct mail, email, and direct distribution as handouts at schools, community organization meetings, GSA and subbasin outreach meetings. 
• Fact Sheets – Fact sheets may be developed and updated for each of the GSP phases and will include a section with an overview of SGMA facts, and development, public review, and implementation timelines. Fact sheets will be available for download on the Pleasant Valley Subbasin GSAs’ individual websites, distributed at public meetings and industry/community organization meetings, and emailed to the Interested Parties Lists and other community organizations’ email distribution lists. 
• Letter Correspondence – When letter correspondence is necessary, particularly during the public review and implementation phases, letters will be distributed via email and/or direct mail. Letters will include pertinent facts and explanations that need to be communicated to specific stakeholder groups. Notification letters will be sent to public agencies and government entities when the GSP is released for the 90-day public review.
 • Presentation Materials – Power Point presentations will be utilized at educational/outreach public meetings. If a Power Point isn’t possible to display for a meeting, display boards printed at 24-inch x 36-inch or larger in size will be used and set up on easels. Handouts of presentations and smaller versions of display boards can be distributed to stakeholders in attendance, emailed to the Interested Parties list, and posted on individual GSAs’ websites for stakeholders to access, particularly if they were unable to attend. 
• Newsletter – Annual or bi-annual newsletters may be created during the GSP implementation phase to inform stakeholders of compliance requirements and groundwater sustainability updates, opportunities, and programs within the Pleasant Valley Subbasin. If developed, the newsletter will be distributed to stakeholders within the subbasin boundary and to those on the Interested Parties Lists, posted on individual GSAs’ websites, and to others who have specific roles during the GSP development, public review and implementation phases. The ideal timeframe to develop and distribute the annual newsletter is following the submittal of the Annual Report each year. 
• Other Printed Materials – Other printed materials may be developed during the GSP development, public review and implementation phases.

3.1.9 General Groundwater Quality
Kenneth D. Schmidt and Associates (KDSA, 2000) have stated that the TDS of groundwater in Pleasant Valley WD ranged from 1,000 to 3,000 mg/L with an average of 1,500 mg/L. The major constituents of concern in groundwater for irrigation use include sodium adsorption ratio, bicarbonate, boron, and pH. More in-depth discussions on groundwater quality are included in this GSP in Section 3.2.5 Groundwater Quality Issues.
&&&
3.2.5 Groundwater Quality Issues
Kahanovitz and Manning (1954) reported that the total dissolved solids (TDS) concentrations ranged from about 1,200 to 2,000 mg/1 and boron concentrations ranged from about 1.5 to 5.2 mg/1 in groundwater in Pleasant Valley as of the 1950s. The alluvium in Pleasant Valley contains significant amounts of gypsum, and much of the groundwater is of the calcium-sodium sulfate type. Hydro Development Inc. (1969) reported on the results of sampling water from a 3,000-feet deep test hole in Section 9, T21S/R15E. The results of this hole indicated that the chemical quality of groundwater below a depth of 1,500 feet and above a depth of 2,400 feet at this location was similar to that pumped from most wells in the valley, which were less than about 1,000 feet deep at that time. Water samples were collected from three depth intervals between 1,874 and 2,427 feet in depth. TDS concentrations ranged from 1,007 to 1,629 mg/1 and were lowest (less than 1,030 mg/1) in samples from above a depth of 2,150 feet. The waters were of the sodium sulfate type, and boron concentrations ranged from 0.6 to 0.8 mg/1. As part of an AB3030 grant funded study, water samples were collected from 16 wells in the valley during 2004-5. Chemical analyses were available for an additional 53 wells. Electrical conductivities of well water were shown in Figure 3-27 by Provost & Pritchard. Electrical conductivities ranged from about 1,600 to 3,560 micromhos/cm at 25°C. These are equivalent to TDS concentrations of about 1,070 to 2,370 mg/l. Moderate problems for micro-plugging of drip irrigation systems can occur when TDS concentrations exceed 500 mg/l, and severe problems can occur when TDS concentrations exceed 2,000 mg/l. The highest ECs were generally in water from wells within a few miles of Jayne Avenue, to the north and south.
Figure 3-28 shows boron concentrations in well water. Boron concentrations ranged from about 0.4 to 3.2 mg/1. The desirable boron concentration for boron sensitive crops is less than 0.5 mg/1. The lowest boron concentrations (0.4 to 0.8 mg/1) were found in water from wells near Zapato Chino Creek. As for TDS, the highest concentrations in water were from wells within about two miles of Jayne Avenue.
Figure 3-29 shows chloride concentrations in well water. Chloride concentrations ranged from 26 to 301 mg/1. The lowest concentrations (26 to 106 mg/1) were found near Zapato Chino Creek. The highest chloride concentrations were in wells within about two miles of Jayne Avenue. The desirable chloride concentration for irrigation for some crops is less than 106 mg/1. The recommended MCL for public water supplies is 500 mg/L. Sulfate concentrations in well water ranged from 251 to 1,910 mg/1 (Figure 3-30). The lowest sulfate concentrations (251 to 500 mg/1) were found in water from wells near Zapato Chino Creek. The highest sulfate concentrations were generally found in water from wells in the west part of the valley. Sulfate is generally not a concern for irrigation. Problems can occur with plugging of drip systems for bicarbonate concentrations exceeding about 120 mg/1. Severe problems can occur when bicarbonate concentrations exceed about 245 mg/1. Of the wells sampled in 2004-05, almost all had bicarbonate concentrations exceeding 120 mg/1. Two of these wells had bicarbonate concentrations exceeding 245 mg/1.
In summary, the inorganic chemical quality of groundwater in Pleasant Valley is unsuitable for public supply and marginal for irrigation of some crops. The groundwater quality is generally suitable for irrigation of pistachios, except for the micro irrigation system plugging hazard. Moderate plugging problems are indicated when TDS concentrations exceed 500 mg/1 and bicarbonate concentrations exceed about 120 mg/1. Severe problems are indicated when TDS concentrations exceed 2,000 mg/1 and bicarbonate concentrations exceed about 245 mg/1.
&&&
Groundwater Quality in Zapato Chino Area
Based on the 2005 water quality study (P&P, 2005), EC and Sulfate were identified as the main conditions of concern with regards to drinking water standards. Boron and Chloride were identified as chemicals of concern with regards to USBR quality standards for acceptance into the Coalinga Cannel, which is principally an issue only if future management plans call for groundwater to be pumped from Pleasant Valley and transported via the canal, which at one time was being considered for a proposed water banking project along
Zapato Chino Creek. This is now considered unlikely due to the area being planted in permanent crops and as surface water is being brought into Pleasant Valley via pipeline for recharge or direct irrigation of crops. At the time, the area along Zapato Chino Creek was a potentially favorable area for recharge as it appeared to be an area of better groundwater quality and was closer to the Coalinga Canal than other areas in Pleasant Valley. As described below, the 2005 water quality study generally indicates that groundwater quality in the area around Zapato Chino Creek is of higher quality than the other areas within the Subbasin.
Electrical Conductivity (EC)
California drinking water standards list an upper secondary MCL of 1,600 micromhos/cm for EC. An EC values map is presented as Figure 3-27. As shown on the map, EC values less than 1,600 micromhos/cm are generally found around Zapato Chino Creek. EC values in other areas of the Subbasin ranged between 1,601 to 3,560 micromhos/cm.
Bicarbonate
One of the most common problems with irrigation water is excess bicarbonates. Bicarbonates get into water when it passes through a calcium carbonate or magnesium carbonate (limestone or dolomite) rock formation. The stone dissolves into calcium and/or  Magnesium ions and bicarbonate ions.
Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR)
High sodium ions in water affects the permeability of soil and causes infiltration problems. This is because sodium when present in the soil in exchangeable form replaces calcium and magnesium adsorbed on the soil clays and causes dispersion of soil particles (i.e., if calcium and magnesium are the predominant cations adsorbed on the soil exchange complex, the soil tends to have a permeable
and granular structure). This dispersion results in the breakdown of soil aggregates. The soil becomes hard and compact when dry and reduces infiltration rates of water and air into the soil affecting its structure. This problem is also related to salinity and type of soil. For example, sandy soils may not get damaged as easily as other heavier soils when it is irrigated with a high SAR water.
pH
Water quality is a key factor affecting pH and nutritional management in crops. Water pH is important for crop management. Water pH affects the solubility of fertilizers and the efficacy of insecticides and fungicides before they are applied to the crop. Generally, the higher the water pH is, then lower the solubility of these materials.
Boron
There are no California drinking water standards for Boron; however, concentrations above 0.5 mg/L have been recognized as detrimental to boron sensitive crops. The USBR Water Quality Standards for Acceptance of Water into the Coalinga Canal is 0.8 mg/L. As shown on Figure 3-28, boron concentrations of 0.4 to 0.8 are generally found around Zapato Chino Creek. Boron concentrations in other areas of the Subbasin ranged between 0.81 to 3.20 mg/L.
Chloride
California drinking water standards list an upper secondary MCL for chloride of 500 mg/L. Concentrations above 105 mg/L have been recognized as detrimental to yield for certain crops such as almonds. The USBR Water Quality Standard for Acceptance of Water into the Coalinga Canal is 106 mg/L. As shown on Figure 3-29, chloride concentrations ranging from 26 to 106 mg/L have been found in groundwater in the general areas around Zapato Chino Creek. Chloride concentrations in other areas of the Subbasin have concentrations that ranged from 107 to 301 mg/L.
Sulfate
California drinking water standards list an upper secondary MCL for sulfate of 500 mg/L. The USBR Water Quality Standard for Acceptance of Water into the Coalinga Canal is 250 mg/L for sulfate. As shown on Figure 3-30, sulfate concentrations ranged from 251 mg/L to 500 mg/L have been detected in groundwater in the general areas around Zapato Chino Creek. Sulfate concentrations in other areas of the Subbasin have concentrations that ranged from 601 to 1,910 mg/L.
&&&
Groundwater Ambient Monitoring and Assessment Program
A review of the California Water Boards GAMA Groundwater Information System (https://gamagroundwater.waterboards.ca.gov/gama/gamamap/public/) indicates that pesticides 1,2- Dibromo-3-chloropropane (DBCP) and 1,2,3-Trichloropropane (1,2,3-TCP), or naturally occurring uranium, are not groundwater issues within the Pleasant Valley Subbasin, as they are within other parts of the San Joaquin Valley.
Lost Hills Community
Fresno County provided information from well permits and subsequently performed one-time sampling of six domestic wells in the rural residential “Lost Hills Community” (Figure 3-27 through Figure 3-30 show location of the Lost Hills Community) at the time of well construction for the years 1993 to 1999. The Lost Hills Community is located along State Route 33 (Lost Hills Road) and approximately 12 miles southeast of  the City of Coalinga and approximately one-mile northwest of the Kings County line. The community includes 172 parcels totaling approximately 162 acres, though only a portion of the parcels are developed. Private wells provide water to the residents of the community and only one well drilling permit was approved for a mobile home from 2009 to 2019. Groundwater analytical results for the sampled domestic wells indicated that chemicals of concern for groundwater in this area included:
 Nitrate as NO3, ranging from 9 mg/L to 64 mg/L (California Drinking Water Standard MCL of 45 mg/L), with one well out of the six sampled exceeding the MCL;
 Sulfate, ranging from 385 mg/L to 926 mg/L (California Drinking Water Standard Secondary MCL of 500 mg/L), with four wells out of the six sampled exceeding the MCL; and
 EC, ranging from 1050 micromhos/cm to 1850 micromhos/cm (California Drinking Water Standard Secondary MCL of 1600 micromhos/cm) with three wells out of the six sampled exceeding the MCL.

As discussed in other parts of this GSP, surface water within the Subbasin is limited to the local intermittent and perennial streams that flow generally from west to east through the Subbasin. Water only flows in the streams after periods of moderate or substantial rainfall, which typically only occurs between November and April. In between rain events the streams are typically dry. As a result, the streams can be dry any time of the year. Records for Los Gatos Creek show that, during significant droughts, no flow occurs for a period of 12 months or more. Groundwater within the Subbasin is generally encountered at depths between 300 to 500 feet below ground. Due to the natural intermittent nature of surface water in the Subbasin and the depths to groundwater, the Subbasin does not have interconnected surface water and groundwater as defined in the SGMA regulations.

Unnamed: 9:
Groundwater Dependent Ecosystems (GDEs) are defined under SGMA as “ecological communities of species that depend on groundwater emerging from aquifers or on groundwater occurring near the ground surface” (23 CCR § 351(m)). While GDEs are not one of the six groundwater conditions that can lead to undesirable results, they can be related to sustainable management criteria identified in Chapter 4. The Nature Conservancy’s Natural Communities Dataset Viewer (https://gis.water.ca.gov/app/NCDatasetViewer/ ) (NC) Vegetative GDE and Wetland GDE map with basin-wide marked revisions is provided below as Figure 3-35. Recognizing that the Subbasin has a depth to groundwater greater than the generally accepted 30-foot proxy for confirming that polygons in the NC dataset are supported by groundwater, the GDEs identified in the NC Dataset Viewer are believed to be largely mischaracterized.

As a conservative approach, Spring 2019 depth to groundwater contours and NC Dataset Viewer GDEs were overlaid to identify NC Dataset Viewer called out GDEs in areas with depth to groundwater greater than 200 feet. By assigning a 200-foot vertical buffer, the assessment accounts for variability in rooting depth exceeding the reported deepest GDE rooting depth of thirty-feet. NC Dataset Viewer-called out GDEs located in areas with groundwater depths exceeding the 200-foot vertical buffer were categorized as “Incorrectly Characterized as a GDE” and are therefore rejected as potential GDEs. The aquifer is thinner near the edges of the Subbasin and the 200-foot vertical buffer may or may not be appropriate in the areas of limited to no information. The Subbasin GSAs will continue to investigate the areas indicated in Figure 3-36 in order to fill the data gap. The areas in Figure 3-36 are the only potential locations for GDEs in the Subbasin.

This document provided estimates for 2030 and 2070. Since 2040 is the deadline for sustainability, and therefore the focus of the water budgets, impacts from 2042 were interpolated between the 2030 and 2070 results. The 2072 conditions were assumed to be the same as 2070 estimates.
The DWR climate change datasets were developed for the California Water Commission’s Water Storage Investment Program (WSIP). As described by DWR, the WSIP dataset is consistent with other DWR programs, is based on best available science, builds on previous efforts, incorporates the latest advances in projections, and follows the Climate Change Technical Advisory Group’s guidance. The available datasets include central tendency projections of ensembles of general circulation models for 2030 and 2070 levels. The datasets also include climatic bookends for 2070 conditions with a drier, extreme warming scenario and a wetter, moderate warming scenario being provided. Only the central tendency simulations were used for preparing the water budgets. For the Subbasin, two DWR datasets were used – projected precipitation and projected evapotranspiration

The “sustainable yield” for the Subbasin is defined as the amount of groundwater pumping that can occur while maintaining groundwater at sustainable levels and avoiding undesirable results. On average, maintaining groundwater pumping at levels that result in no long-term storage declines is used as the initial estimate of sustainable yield. The sustainable yield can be estimated as the Total Groundwater Recharge (from natural and artificial sources) minus the Groundwater Outflow. The Groundwater Recharge would include all of the recharge components shown in Table 3-8.
Using the change in levels methodology the sustainable yield is estimated at 23,500 AF.
Using the Current Water Budget (Table 3-8), the sustainable yield for the Subbasin is estimated to be:
22,577 AF – 0 AF = 22,577 AF/year, which approximates the current groundwater pumping (43,300 AF/year) minus the overdraft (19,023 AF/year) and the groundwater outflow (1,700 AF/year).

The goal of this GSP is to start to slow the decline in water levels over time as quickly as possible. To this end it is not the intent to set Management Objectives or Minimum thresholds; however, based upon the plan to lessen the rate of decline of water levels, interim milestones, management objectives, and minimum thresholds have been identified based upon the plan to reduce the rate of decline in water levels. The goal is to lessen the rate of decline in water levels in the basin. To this end the following is the initial goal of this GSP: Existing rate of decline in water levels 10 ft/yr
2025 Goal <7 ft/yr
2030 Goal <5 ft/yr
2042 Goal 0 ft/yr
A graph of what this transition might look like is shown in Figure 4-2 below

4.2.2.1 Criteria to Define Undesirable Results
Undesirable results related to groundwater level declines in the Subbasin would be a signal of diminished groundwater supplies for agricultural, municipal, industrial, and domestic needs. Agriculture is the main economic enterprise of the Subbasin, so effective management of groundwater for sustainable future use is critical to the continuation of current economic interests which add value to the Subbasin’s communities. Declines in groundwater levels will increase the energy needed for pumping. Continued groundwater level declines have the potential to negatively impact well capacity, requiring deepening and/or replacement to produce the desired amount of water. Continued groundwater level declines could also force some well owners to lower or replace existing pumps if the existing well casing is sufficiently deep. Although all of these potential impacts can be mitigated technically, they are considered undesirable results due to the expense involved.
&&&
4.5.1 Undesirable Results
Current salinity data available in the region indicates EC measurements in groundwater ranges between 1,601 to 3,560 micromhos/cm for most of the Subbasin. The southern portion of the Subbasin near the Zapato Chino Creek community has EC measurements below 1,600 micromhos/cm, which is the secondary MCL for EC. There is a small community at the southern end of the basin that relies on groundwater, shown in Figure 4-10. With the planned import of surface supplies, it is expected that there will be a slight positive impact on groundwater quality, which will continue to be monitored for changes. The importation of surface supplies is planned to include large volumes of intentional recharge. Monitor wells would be incorporated into the design of recharge basins to allow for testing for water quality.
An Undesirable Result related to groundwater quality is defined as the significant and unreasonable degradation of water quality to be determined by the Subbasin and its constituents. Stakeholder meetings are held with the Pleasant Valley Subbasin to determine what is significant and unreasonable locally. Therefore, an Undesirable Result related to groundwater quality in the Subbasin is defined as the degradation of groundwater quality such that pistachios can no longer grow. It has been recognized that the City of Coalinga and surrounding municipal demands have been served surface water due to the inability of the groundwate to meet the requirements and demands of human consumption. There is a data gap for the small Lost Hills Community in the southern portion of the subbasin. It is unknown the quality of the groundwater that is developed by individual wells that serve these residences. It is expected that it is of similar quality found in the remainder of the Subbasin, and during the first 5-year implementation window, more information will be developed to understand the quality of the water serving this community.
&&&
4.5.1.1 Criteria to Define Undesirable Results
Undesirable Results would be the continual degradation of supplies that would not allow for the continued farming of pistachios and other salt tolerant crops. The criteria relied upon to define this result includes historical groundwater conditions related to water quality and the potential for future use. According to Ferguson, Sanden, & Grattan (2010), the groundwater will no longer provide beneficial use to pistachios when salt concentrations or TDS reach a level causing EC to measure more than 5,000 micromhos/cm or higher.

The goal of this GSP is to start to slow the decline in water levels over time as quickly as possible. To this end it is not the intent to set Management Objectives or Minimum thresholds; however, based upon the plan to lessen the rate of decline of water levels, interim milestones, management objectives, and minimum thresholds have been identified based upon the plan to reduce the rate of decline in water levels. 2042 Goal 0 ft/yr

The process for establishing the MTs for the groundwater levels sustainability indicator for the Subbasin is based on the following concepts, each of which was developed using the information developed for the HCM and
as described in other portions of the GSP. 1. Groundwater levels in the Subbasin are generally declining, though at different rates in different locations in the Subbasin. 2. The continued decline of groundwater levels in the Subbasin may result in impacts to beneficial uses and users that are significant and unreasonable or undesirable. If this were to occur, it may reach a point where the GSAs may not be able to manage and/or mitigate the effects, so a proactive approach needs to be implemented now. 3. The GSAs of the Subbasin will be implementing projects and management actions designed to reduce the rate of groundwater level decline in the Subbasin and eventually stabilize groundwater levels into the future (Chapter 6). - Implementation of the projects and management actions by the GSAs is anticipated to begin in 2023 and be completed by 2040 (Chapter 7). 4. The results of forecast simulations indicate that the implementation of the projects and management actions by the GSAs proposed in this GSP will begin to reduce the rate of groundwater level decline in the Subbasin by 2025 and will stabilize groundwater levels by 2042. Using the above as a guide, MTs for the groundwater levels sustainability indicator in the Subbasin were established using historical data for RMS wells as follows.
1. RMS sites were chosen based on availability of known well information, location in the GSA, and availability of historical data.
2. Groundwater levels for each RMS site in the Subbasin were graphed for the entire history of recorded data.
3. The estimated groundwater elevation in 2042, based on the current pumping patterns or the last 15 years of elevation data, is the MT at that RMS. The assumptions include: - Lands that are currently in production continue to stay in production. - Water use, surface water deliveries, etc. in the Subbasin generally remain the same into the future. This is considered a reasonable assumption given the current groundwater conditions in the Subbasin. It is anticipated that there will be a slight increase in demands due to the maturing of several pistachio plantings in the Subbasin. An example hydrograph illustrating this process is shown in Figure 4-3. Hydrographs at all the RMSs or substitute locations showing the MTs, MOs, and stabilized groundwater levels by 2042 are presented in Figure 4-3 through Figure 4-8. The MT at each RMS is listed in Table 4-2.

Groundwater level measurements will be collected every winter and fall to provide data on the seasonal low and seasonal high groundwater conditions. 6.2.1 Project #PV 01 North Pipeline Project
The proposed project is for construction of a 60 cfs pump station and water pipeline and an ecological restoration for groundwater recharge near the city of Coalinga. The project has two parts: 1) Pumpstation and main pipeline from the Coalinga Canal to Los Gatos Creek, including diversion pipelines into the creek and 2) the construction of a recharge and restoration (R&R) area in and around Los Gatos Creek. Construction for the pump station and the pipeline is planned for the months ofOctober to March, when the Coalinga Canal has minimal use, actual construction year will be based on coordination with the Bureau. The entire area of potential effect (APE) is 264 acres (90 for the main pipeline and 174 acres for the Los Gatos Creek area).
&&&
6.2.3 Project #PV 03 Los Gatos Creek Gravel Pits Recharge Project
The following groundwater recharge project is to capture seasonal surface water flows in Los Gatos Creek as it flows past Granite’s Coalinga, California operations. The overall project would divert surface water flows from Los Gatos Creek into an existing gravel pit that is not currently being used just west of the airport runway. Evaluation of the potential for groundwater recharge via existing gravel pits at the Granite Construction Company Site in the City of Coalinga was evaluated in 2003 by Provost & Pritchard in the report, “Pleasant Valley Water District Groundwater Storage Pilot Project and Feasibility Study”. In that report a groundwater recharge project at Granite was found to be a potentially viable project to provide water storage in the aquifer below Pleasant Valley for subsequent extraction. It includes a concrete turnout structure and a pipeline that feeds into the gravel pit. An estimated volume of 960 AF can be stored in the pit at any given time. The infiltration rate is predicted to be in the range between 2 and 4 feet per day.
&&&

Unnamed: 9:
The proposed project is for construction of a 60 cfs pump station and water pipeline and an ecological restoration for groundwater recharge near the city of Coalinga. The project has two parts: 1) Pumpstation and main pipeline from the Coalinga Canal to Los Gatos Creek, including diversion pipelines into the creek and 2) the construction of a recharge and restoration (R&R) area in and around Los Gatos Creek. Construction for the pump station and the pipeline is planned for the months of October to March, when the Coalinga Canal has minimal use, actual construction year will be based on coordination with the Bureau. The entire area of potential effect (APE) is 264 acres (90 for the main pipeline and 174 acres for the Los Gatos Creek area)...
...It is estimated that the projects estimated average annual benefit is 12,000 AF/yr. Based on 100 diversion days. The project can be used throughout the year when water is available.
&&
This project proposes to expand the current pumping capacity out of the Coalinga canal, with an increase of 20 cfs and to connect to the existing 40 cfs booster pump station. The local landowners have been proactive and have funded and permitted the existing project. The project participants have permitted the physical improvements and it is anticipated that no additional permits will be required. This project also proposes to extend the pipeline an additional 2 miles as a lateral off one of the main lines to deliver water to areas in need...
...It is estimated that the projects estimated annual benefit is 4,000 AF/yr. Based on 100 diversion days. The project can be used throughout the year when water is available.
&&
The following groundwater recharge project is to capture seasonal surface water flows in Los Gatos Creek as it flows past Granite’s Coalinga, California operations. The overall project would divert surface water flows from Los Gatos Creek into an existing gravel pit that is not currently being used just west of the airport runway. Evaluation of the potential for groundwater recharge via existing gravel pits at the Granite Construction Company Site in the City of Coalinga was evaluated in 2003 by Provost & Pritchard in the report, “Pleasant Valley Water District Groundwater Storage Pilot Project and Feasibility Study”. In that report a groundwater recharge project at Granite was found to be a potentially viable project to provide water storage in the aquifer below Pleasant Valley for subsequent extraction. It includes a concrete turnout structure and a pipeline that feeds into the gravel pit. An estimated volume of 960 AF can be stored in the pit at any given time. The infiltration rate is predicted to be in the range between 2 and 4 feet per day...
...It is anticipated that during the wet part of the year that the volume can be refilled twice in a wet year and once in a normal year. A cycle of a wet, normal and two dry years are used for this analysis. Average estimated benefit is 720 AF/year.
&&
This project will utilize various techniques to slow the instream flow and allow water ponding within the stream to increase the duration of water contact with the soil to increase infiltration amount within the stream. The flow in the channel is typically at a depth of 2-5 feet deep, while the channel is in places 10 15 feet deep. The goal would be to design swales within the channel to increase the depth of water but not impact or restrict the channel capacity during a high flow...
...The estimate annual project benefit is based on the stream running in three out of five years. The increased area expected to be 5 acres. The infiltration rate in the stream is in the range of 9 cubic feet per second to 15 cubic feet per second per mile and is expected to increase the duration of the flow to 12 hours for a total average yearly benefit in the range of 50 AF/yr to 75 AF/yr.
&&
Project is to construct 130 acres of infiltration basins on section 31 to recharge ground water to recover it in dry years. The infiltration rate in this area is estimated to range from 0.5 to 1.0 ft per day. Utilizing the North Pipeline to supply water to this project. The basins will be shallow in nature with 1 to 2 feet of cut material...
...The estimate annual project benefit is based on the water being available from an outside source 3 out 5 years and the project can be utilized for a minimum of 30 days in a normal water year. The average yearly benefit can range from 4,700 to 9,400 AF/yr.

Unnamed: 9: GSAs have a variety of tools that can be used to achieve sustainable groundwater management, which fall into two primary categories – 1) project development for water supply augmentation and 2) management actions for demand reduction. A combination of these actions will likely be needed to achieve sustainability. The projects described herein focus on the development, use, and recharge of available surface water supplies to reduce the lowering of the groundwater levels. Management actions have been developed that focus on reducing water demand and associated reduction of groundwater pumping, along with increased data collection and associated actions including education and outreach, regulatory policies, incentive-based programs, and enforcement actions.

Unnamed: 9: Groundwater conditions leading to Undesirable Results in relation to groundwater quality would be significant lowering of groundwater levels to the point at which the water becomes too high in total dissolved solids. This may occur if pumping restrictions are not followed or if surface water supplies are not developed. Climate change is also a concern for groundwater conditions. If drought years persist, groundwater levels may drop at faster rates than normal.
Unnamed: 11: Climate change identified as a potential driver of Lowering of GW Levels and Reduction of GW Storage (not directly stated, but drivers of UR are the same for both indicators). As a result of UR for these SMCs, water quality could also be affected by climate change.

Unnamed: 9: The Irrigated Lands Regulatory Program (ILRP) was initiated in 2003 to address pollutant discharges to surface water and groundwater from commercially irrigated lands. The primary purpose of the ILRP is to address key pollutants of concern including salinity, nitrates, and pesticides introduced through runoff or infiltration of irrigation water and stormwater. Surface water quality has been monitored for several years, and in the future groundwater quality will also be monitored. The program is administered by the Central Valley Regional Water Quality Control Board (RWQCB).
